beats.activity: Waking up, making tea, and journaling at home in the soft morning light.
beats.reasoning: Maya is an early riser who values morning ritual; she journaled yesterday and will do so again today. Starting at 6:15am aligns with her calm, intentional personality.

beats.activity: Walking to Gas Works Park for sunrise and light stretching despite the air quality warning.
beats.reasoning: Maya loves rainy mornings and nature walks; yesterday she walked Gas Works despite drizzle. Today it's clear and hot—perfect conditions. She'll go despite moderate smoke, as the morning light is her favorite time. 45-min walk from Fremont.

beats.activity: Commuting from Gas Works Park back to Fremont, then driving to CorePower Yoga in Capitol Hill.
beats.reasoning: She needs to return home to freshen up before her morning class. Total travel time ~45 min accounts for the walk back to Fremont and drive across town to Capitol Hill.

beats.activity: Teaching a morning vinyasa class at CorePower Yoga.; Transition time at the studio: resetting the room, hydrating well (heat warning today), and prepping for the next class.; Teaching a mid-morning power yoga class.
beats.reasoning: Yoga instructor teaching schedule; morning classes are typical. She's energized from her park walk and will bring that presence to students.

beats.activity: Grabbing a smoothie bowl and sitting mindfully, avoiding screens—her favorite reset between classes.
beats.reasoning: Maya loves smoothie bowls and deliberately avoids screens before noon. She did this yesterday and will repeat today. This is her grounding ritual mid-teaching day.

beats.activity: Teaching a noon power class in peak heat conditions.; Wrapping up at the studio, hydrating, and preparing to head home early to avoid the afternoon peak heat and smoke.
beats.reasoning: Third teaching block. Heat will affect the studio and student energy; she'll stay hydrated and attentive to the room's needs.

beats.activity: Driving home to Fremont in the heat, staying hydrated.
beats.reasoning: 45-min drive from Capitol Hill to Fremont. She's leaving mid-afternoon to avoid the worst heat and will rest at home.

beats.activity: Resting at home: stretching on her mat, drinking water and electrolytes, light reading, and staying cool indoors during peak heat.
beats.reasoning: Maya's low energy from yesterday (40/100) carries into today. The extreme heat and air quality make staying home the right choice. She'll recover with gentle stretching and rest.

beats.activity: Walking to Gas Works Park for the free outdoor concert at 6pm; light dinner/snack beforehand.
beats.reasoning: Evening has cooled slightly; the concert is a low-key social outing that doesn't require screens or intense energy. She loves this park and can enjoy live music outdoors once the worst heat passes.

beats.activity: Enjoying the free outdoor concert at Gas Works Park with the community.
beats.reasoning: World event: outdoor concert draws crowds. Maya's calm nature appreciates live music and community gatherings. She'll stay for the full set, then head home as it cools.

beats.activity: Walking home from Gas Works Park, enjoying the cooled evening air.
beats.reasoning: Easy 40-min walk back to Fremont from the park. Evening is now pleasant; she'll decompress on the walk.

beats.activity: Evening wind-down: gentle stretching on her mat, herbal tea, and journaling about the day and concert.
beats.reasoning: Maya's evening ritual: reflection and gentle movement. She'll prepare for sleep with her grounding practice.

beats.activity: Sleeping deeply after a full day of teaching and community time.
beats.reasoning: 7.5 hours of sleep, ending at 6:30am tomorrow. She'll wake early again and repeat her sunrise ritual.

diary: Started the day the way I like to—tea and journaling in that golden light coming through my Fremont windows. Couldn't resist the sunrise at Gas Works even with the smoke haze; something about that ritual grounds me before the chaos. The studio was intense today. Three classes back-to-back in 91-degree heat meant I was basically coaching people through survival mode, which somehow felt important? Everyone was pushing harder because of the weather, maybe. Grabbed my smoothie bowl at Solstice and just sat there, phone off, which saved me. By noon I could feel the heat getting to me, so I headed home early to actually rest instead of pretending. That decision felt right. The concert at Gas Works tonight was perfect—smaller crowd than I expected, probably the smoke keeping people away, but that made it better. Walked home as the air cooled down, feeling tired in that good way.
thought_process: I made the call early to prioritize my body today. The heat warning and air quality could've felt like a nuisance, but I reframed it as a sign to listen harder to what I needed—which is kind of what I teach anyway. The morning park ritual kept me centered even though the smoke was visible. Teaching through the peak heat pushed me to focus on hydration and pacing my students well, not just getting through classes. Skipping the afternoon rush and resting at home felt countercultural for a yoga instructor, but I knew I'd be useless if I burned out. The concert ended up being restorative rather than draining because fewer people meant more space, more presence. My mood shifted from 'managing the heat' to 'using the heat as information' about what my body actually wanted.
world_event_prompt: Hot, clear Wednesday hitting 91F by mid-afternoon. Free outdoor concert at Gas Works Park from 6pm draws big crowds. Moderate air quality from distant BC wildfire smoke.
